marketOverview: Global markets navigate heightened geopolitical tensions around US-Iran conflict escalation, shifting Fed expectations after hawkish pivot from Governor Miran, and persistent trade uncertainty under Section 122 tariffs. Dollar strength dominates as US yields firm on strong data and safe-haven demand combines with rate differentials. European growth remains subdued despite German manufacturing returning to expansion, with Lagarde succession speculation adding political risk. Commodity currencies split sharply: AUD supported by hawkish RBA and resilient labor market, NZD pressured by RBNZ's dovish pivot under new Governor Breman. JPY caught between softer domestic normalization and intermittent safe-haven flows.

currencies.name: US Dollar
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver remains the relative rates channel and safe haven demand tied to geopolitical risk. Strong US data, including a hotter Core PCE print at 3.0% YoY, combined with Fed Miran's hawkish pivot from 150bps to 100bps of expected cuts have firmed US front end yields and supported the Dollar. US-Iran tensions, with a 15-day deadline for military action, reinforce safe-haven flows, and the USD benefits from combining defensive demand with a yield advantage over peers. Trade policy uncertainty under Section 122 tariffs adds volatility but has not derailed Dollar strength.

currencies.name: Euro
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver has shifted back toward a firmer US rates impulse and renewed external risk rather than a stabilized ECB front end. Even though German Manufacturing PMI surged to 50.7, returning to expansion for the first time in over three and a half years, and the Eurozone Composite PMI beat expectations at 51.9, EUR could not sustain gains as higher US two year yields and a more hawkish Fed tone reasserted dollar support. Lagarde succession speculation has added a political risk discount and kept ECB pricing cautious at the margin, limiting the relative rate channel that previously helped the Euro. Trade escalation under Section 122 reintroduces export sensitivity for a bloc still reliant on external demand, particularly Germany.
drivers: German Manufacturing PMI 50.7 (first expansion in 3.5 years)
ECB Negotiated Wages Q4 3.0% (slightly above exp)
EU sanctions package against Russia stalled

currencies.name: British Pound
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver remains the easing bias at the BOE, even if this week's data complicated the picture. Labor market deterioration, with unemployment rising to 5.2% and wage growth cooling to 4.2%, reinforces the view that underlying momentum is softening and validates markets pricing a high probability of a near term cut at 88-95%. While Core CPI at 3.1% and Services inflation at 4.4% are still elevated, the direction of travel in wages and Catherine Mann's emphasis on sluggish growth tilt the reaction function toward easing. Strong Retail Sales at +1.8% MoM and steady Services PMI at 53.9 show resilience but have not pushed back meaningfully against rate cut expectations.
drivers: UK-US tension over Diego Garcia base

currencies.name: Japanese Yen
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver has swung back toward a softer domestic normalization impulse rather than an imminent tightening cycle. The downside surprise in Q4 GDP and the softer January CPI print, with headline at 1.5% YoY versus 1.6% expected and supercore measures easing, have reduced conviction around a March hike and allowed markets to reprice BOJ patience. While former officials continue to flag a reasonable probability of a move in March or April, incoming data have not validated urgency, and that limits upward pressure on JGB yields. Externally, US yields have firmed and the Dollar has regained traction, re-widening the rate differential channel. Safe haven demand linked to geopolitical risk has not been sufficient to offset that dynamic, and Japanese equities' resilience reinforces a carry friendly backdrop.
drivers: FOMC minutes confirmed USD/JPY rate checks

currencies.name: Australian Dollar
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver remains the domestic rates impulse, and this week reinforced rather than diluted it. The labor market report delivered a goldilocks outcome, with employment up 17.8k and unemployment steady at 4.1% versus 4.2% expected, with the entire gain from full time positions, confirming that slack is not building in a way that would justify early easing. The RBA minutes underscored that uncertainty is too high to signal a clear pivot and highlighted that inflation would have been stickier without prior hikes, reinforcing a reluctance to join the global easing cycle. Externally, three month LME Copper trending higher and a constructive risk tone into the USD selloff allowed AUD to express its high beta profile cleanly. With front end yields relatively supported and sentiment stabilizing, the path of least resistance into CPI tilts higher across the basket.
drivers: RBA minutes hawkish (no clear pivot signal)
Three month LME Copper trending higher
Risk-on impulse favoring high beta FX

currencies.name: Canadian Dollar
currencies.rationale: The main driver remains the trade and political risk premium tied to the United States, which continues to dominate over oil support and a steady Bank of Canada stance. Headlines around a potential rethink of the USMCA structure in favor of bilateral deals reinforced Canada's external vulnerability, and with over 70% of exports going to the US this channel carries structural weight. Softer CPI at 2.3% YoY versus 2.4% expected and flat retail volumes reduce the urgency for the BoC to lean hawkish, keeping front end yield differentials biased against CAD as US yields push higher. While WTI strength linked to Middle East tensions, rising from $62 to $66, has provided a cushion, oil driven support can fade quickly if supply headlines shift, and CAD's high beta to both oil and risk sentiment leaves it exposed.

currencies.name: Swiss Franc
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver remains the global risk and yield differential channel, and this week reinforced that CHF struggles to attract sustained demand outside of brief volatility spikes. Soft Q4 GDP at 0.2% versus 0.3% expected and contracting Q4 Industrial Production at -0.7% after prior 2.4% underline a fragile domestic backdrop, while structurally low inflation keeps Swiss yields anchored well below peers. In calmer risk conditions, that leaves CHF unattractive on carry, especially as US yields have firmed and the Dollar combines haven demand with a yield advantage. Even during geopolitical tension around Iran and tariff headlines, CHF underperformed USD and JPY, suggesting its safe haven bid is secondary and inconsistent. Rising sight deposits to CHF 437bn and subdued SNB communication signal that policy tolerance for strength remains limited.
drivers: Swiss domestic bank sight deposits rose to CHF 437bn

currencies.name: New Zealand Dollar
currencies.rationale: The dominant driver has shifted decisively to a softer domestic rates channel after the RBNZ's dovish pivot under new Governor Breman. By signaling that the OCR is likely to remain at current levels in the near term and only potentially rise in late 2026, while describing policy as accommodative, the Bank has reopened a clear easing bias and undercut front end yield support. In her debut, Governor Breman said the RBNZ is not planning to hike until it sees a stronger economy and more inflationary pressure, a sharp contrast to the more hawkish tone from key peers. NZD/USD fell hard on the decision, sliding from 0.6042 to below 0.5970, forcing a technical breakdown through 0.5995. Externally, the widening Trans Tasman yield spread in Australia's favor and thin liquidity due to China's Lunar New Year holiday reduced the usual China proxy channel.
drivers: Governor Breman: no hike until stronger economy
OCR to remain at current levels near term
Trans Tasman yield spread widening vs AUD
